reason: The paper reports "Fitted variable importance plots" (Fig 6) generated directly from ranger and xgboost's built-in importance (no mention anywhere in the text of "permutation," "SHAP," "Boruta," or "conditional" importance), i.e. the default impurity/gain-type measure for both packages. This ranking is used to state specific, non-hedged conclusions: "spatial pattern of soil pH is primarily influenced by precipitation and surface reflectance," "depth emerges as the most important covariate," and "DEM-parameters...emerge as second-most dominant covariates" for soil types/textures. Corroboration offered is only qualitative ("largely correspond with conventional soil survey knowledge"), a handful of raw bivariate scatter plots (Fig 7, not partial-dependence/ALE controlling for other covariates), and overall cross-validated R-square (which validates predictive accuracy of the full model, not the selected feature ranking) — none of which meets the bar of permutation/SHAP/PDP/conditional-importance/held-out validation of the ranking itself. The mismatch the authors themselves note (lithology visibly shapes predictions yet falls outside the top-15 importance list) is consistent with the classic impurity-importance artifact of under-weighting lower-cardinality categorical covariates relative to fine-grained continuous ones like precipitation/temperature/reflectance, so the bias could plausibly have altered this specific, stated ranking-based conclusion.
